nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reef slopes, typically at depths of 18–120 m; tends to be found deeper than many aquarium anthias

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
refugium with copepods and amphipods recommended

diet.notes: Generally feeds on zooplankton in the wild. In captivity has been found to accept frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and high-quality marine flake or pellet foods. A refugium producing copepods and amphipods is commonly recommended to supplement feedings, particularly during the acclimation period.

avoidWith: fish that outcompete for food at high speed

summary: A deep-water shoaling anthias from the Indo-West Pacific, generally considered advanced due to acclimation sensitivity. Tends to do best kept in small groups with ample live-rock hiding places and supplemental zooplankton feeding.
careNotes:
Red Saddled Anthias tend to be collected from depths up to ~98 feet (30 m), so newly acquired specimens are often sensitive to shipping stress and commonly advised to be introduced to low-to-moderate lighting initially. Acclimation has been reported as challenging; keepers generally recommend introducing this species before more active or territorial tankmates. A refugium producing copepods and amphipods is widely suggested to ease the transition to prepared foods.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite: all individuals are born female, and if a dominant male is lost, the largest female in a group has been documented transitioning to male. Keeping a small shoal (generally 3+ individuals) with one male and multiple females is considered more natural and tends to reduce stress-related losses.

Temperature sources show some variation: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) while FishBase wild-capture data indicates a mean of 27.2°C with a range of 24.2–28.3°C. The values used here (23.2–27.0°C) represent an average across both sources. Keepers in cooler-running systems (24–25°C) have kept this species successfully, but the FishBase data suggests tolerance of warmer temperatures typical of tropical reefs.

Note on scientific name: This species was formerly placed in genus Pseudanthias but has been reclassified to Pyronotanthias by FishBase (as of 2026). Both names appear in the aquarium trade; search under Pseudanthias flavoguttatus will still surface most husbandry resources.
breedingNotes: Not commonly bred in captivity. Protogynous hermaphroditism means sex ratios in a group are dynamic; the dominant fish will transition to male if no male is present. Spawning in the wild involves broadcast egg release. Captive breeding attempts are not well-documented in the hobbyist literature.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden; could not access. Fell back to FishBase as secondary source.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (10.2 cm male, 8.2 cm female), temperature range (24.2–28.3°C), native distribution, and taxonomy. FishBase lists the current accepted name as Pyronotanthias flavoguttatus. Provides wild-capture temperature data used to average against LiveAquaria's hobby recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: Not commonly bred in captivity; protogynous hermaphrodite. No established captive breeding protocol documented in hobbyist literature.
